title: A 2,000-Year-Old Crime Scene: Fingerprint in Tar Could Unmask Ancient Raiders
summary: For over two millennia, the identity of seaborne raiders who attacked the Danish island of Als has been lost to history. Now, a single fingerprint—pressed into the black caulking tar of a sunken boat—might finally expose them. The Hjortspring boat, discovered in a bog in the 1880s and excavated in the 1920s, is the only prehistoric plank boat ever found in Scandinavia. It was likely used in a fourth-century B.C.E. raid that ended in defeat: islanders sank one of the attackers' vessels, possibly as a victory offering. Recently, researchers from Lund University re-examined un-preserved fragments of the boat using modern techniques. 'The boat was waterproofed with pine pitch, which was surprising,' said archaeologist Mikael Fauvelle. 'This suggests the boat was built somewhere with abundant pine forests.' Previously, scholars believed the raiders originated from modern-day Hamburg, Germany. But the pine pitch—identified through chromatography and mass spectrometry—points instead to the Baltic Sea region east of Rügen and Scania. 'If the boat came from the pine forest-rich coastal regions of the Baltic Sea, it means the warriors who attacked the island of Als chose to launch a maritime raid over hundreds of kilometers of open sea,' Fauvelle said. The team also used X-ray tomography to create a 3D model of the fingerprint, and carbon-dated lime bast cordage to confirm the boat's pre-Roman Iron Age age. They even hope to extract ancient DNA from the tar, which could provide unprecedented details about the raiders themselves. 'Together, these results shed new light on the methods and materials used to build Scandinavia's first plank boats,' the authors wrote in PLOS One, 'and raise new questions regarding our understanding of early maritime societies in Northern Europe.' The fingerprint is a direct, haunting link to an individual who lived and fought 2,400 years ago—a tiny clue that might rewrite a chapter of prehistoric warfare.
why_it_matters: This story shows that even a tiny fingerprint can change how we understand prehistoric warfare and seafaring. It demonstrates that with the right technology, seemingly silent artifacts can speak, and might encourage you to look at history as a puzzle you can help solve through science, technology, and careful reasoning.

keywords.explanation: A sticky waterproof material, often made from pine pitch, used to seal the gaps between wooden planks on a boat.
A scientific method that separates different chemicals in a mixture so they can be identified one by one.
A technique that measures the mass of molecules to determine exactly what a substance is made of.
An imaging method that takes many X-ray pictures from different angles to build a 3D model of an object without having to cut it open.
Rope made from the inner bark fiber of a lime (linden) tree, used on the prehistoric boat and later carbon-dated to confirm its age.
The period in Northern Europe roughly from 500 to 1 BC, before the Roman Empire expanded into the region, when iron tools were already in use.

questions.question: Where was the Hjortspring boat discovered?
options: In a bog on the island of Als in Denmark
At the bottom of the Baltic Sea
Inside a Viking burial mound in Scania
questions.correct_answer: In a bog on the island of Als in Denmark

questions.question: What surprising material did researchers identify in the boat's waterproofing?
Where do scientists now believe the boat was most likely built?
options: Baltic Sea region east of Rügen and Scania
questions.correct_answer: Baltic Sea region east of Rügen and Scania

questions.question: Which technique was used to create a 3D model of the fingerprint?
What was carbon-dated to confirm the boat's age?
What do researchers hope to extract from the tar in the future?

background_read: The Hjortspring boat is the oldest plank-built boat ever found in Scandinavia, dating to around 350 BC. It was discovered in a peat bog on the Danish island of Als, where the low-oxygen conditions preserved the wood and tar. Archaeologists believe the boat was part of a large war offering — after a local victory, the islanders sank at least one of the attackers' vessels.

Rügen (the largest German island, located in the Baltic Sea) and Scania (the southernmost tip of Sweden) were part of the same pine-forest-rich coastal zone in early Iron Age times. These areas were separated from Als by hundreds of kilometers of open water, so the raiders undertook a risky long-distance maritime journey.

The study was published in 2025 in the journal PLOS One by a team from Lund University (one of Sweden's oldest and largest research universities). Archaeological analysis has advanced enormously: what was once just a black lump of tar can now be scanned with X-ray tomography to reveal fingerprints that have survived for more than two millennia.
Article_Structure: LEAD: A single fingerprint in the caulking tar of a 2,400-year-old sunken boat could identify the seaborne raiders who attacked the island of Als.
└─ quote: 'a direct, haunting link to an individual who lived and fought.'
DISCOVERY CONTEXT: The Hjortspring boat, found in a bog in the 1880s and excavated in the 1920s, is the only prehistoric plank boat in Scandinavia; it was probably sunk as a victory offering.
RE-EXAMINATION: Lund University researchers used modern techniques on un-preserved fragments.
├─ MATERIAL CLUE: Pine pitch identified via chromatography and mass spectrometry indicates a construction origin in a pine-rich region — not Hamburg, but the Baltic Sea east of Rügen and Scania.
├─ FINGERPRINT: X-ray tomography created a 3D model of a preserved fingerprint in the tar.
└─ DATING: Carbon-dated lime bast cordage confirmed the boat's pre-Roman Iron Age age.
QUOTE: 'These results shed new light... and raise new questions regarding our understanding of early maritime societies.'
OPEN QUESTION: Can ancient DNA be extracted from the tar to reveal details about the raiders themselves?

perspectives.description: The find gives a rare personal trace of a person from 2,400 years ago, shedding light on boat-building techniques and long-distance raiding. It could rewrite the map of early Iron Age power in Northern Europe.

perspectives.perspective: Negative / Critical – Limitations of a Single Clue
perspectives.description: One fingerprint may not be enough to identify an entire group; the raiders could have captured the boat from another region. Over-relying on a tiny trace risks building a narrative around too little evidence.

The study illustrates how combining chemistry (chromatography, mass spectrometry) with imaging (X-ray tomography) and dating (carbon-14) can extract information from old material that earlier archaeologists could only guess at.

If scientists succeed in extracting ancient DNA from the tar, they could learn the raider's ancestry, diet, and disease history. This could lead to re-examining other bog finds worldwide with the same techniques.
